global.title: Swarm Channel Architecture

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: Top-level map: splits the Swarm integration into bee-reactor-adapter, SwarmChannel, and a plugin layer each cluster note refines.
Defines the IChannel contract that makes SyncManager transport-agnostic — the seam every other SwarmChannel note plugs into.
Pure-transport constraint: ordering and conflict resolution stay in the reactor, keeping the channel simple and the reactor authoritative.
Explains the inbox/outbox/deadLetter mailbox triad that the SwarmChannel push/pull cycles drive.
Multi-remote per collection is the reactor feature that lets Swarm coexist with GQL for dual sync.
Describes the push/pull data path: ordinal batches going out, manifest traversal coming in.
Procedural detail of the push cycle: serialize, AES-encrypt, upload to /bytes, then write feed update.
Procedural detail of the pull cycle: poll user+doc manifests, decrypt batches into the inbox.
End-to-end sync flow narrative — useful as an orientation read when debugging sync regressions.
Frames Connect as a bridge between Switchboard and Swarm ecosystems — the cross-domain role this channel fills.
Connection state derives from Bee /health — the Settings UI feedback surface tied to this channel.
createSwarmSyncBuilder is the ReactorBuilder hook that wires SwarmChannel into the host reactor.
Decision that the builder path replaces monkey-patching — keeps the channel installable as a plugin.

global.description: How Connect speaks to Swarm as a reactor IChannel: the three-layer split, pure-transport role, and the push/pull cycles that keep PGlite and feeds aligned.
global.orientation: The Swarm Channel is Connect's decentralized sync transport: a reactor IChannel implementation that routes operations through Bee feeds and /bytes instead of Switchboard GraphQL. Notes in this MOC document the three-layer split (bee-reactor-adapter, SwarmChannel, plugin), the IChannel mailbox contract that keeps the channel pure-transport, and the paired push/pull cycles that encrypt, upload, publish, and later decrypt operation batches. The cluster also covers multi-remote reactor support, the bridge role between Switchboard and Swarm, and the debounced health/status surface. Together these notes describe the full architecture that Path A depends on — the plugin-resident transport the user-confirmed end state requires.
